Title: Life EV officially owns Rad Power Bikes now
Author: Kirsten Korosec
Summary: Life EV acquired Rad Power Bikes for $13.2 million after its bankruptcy, gaining IP, inventory, and assets. The company plans to maintain Rad Power’s operations and expand retail in key U.S. markets, supporting existing customers. Life EV, based in Florida, focuses on acquiring and scaling e-bike brands and aims to expand across North America.
Content:
Life Electric Vehicles Holdings, also known as Life EV, officially owns the intellectual property, inventory, and certain operating assets of Rad Power Bikes. Life EV acquired Rad Power for $13.2 million. Rad Power Bikes, a buzzy electric bike company that raised nearly $330 million in venture capital, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in December. The company had struggled for months prior to its bankruptcy filing and had warned employees it might have to shut down without new capital.

Life EV intends to keep the company intact and said it will continue retail operations under the Rad Power Bikes brand in the United States. It also plans to expand the retail footprint in select key markets. Life EV pledged to support existing customers who might be wondering what would become of their bike warranties or gift cards.

The Florida-based Life EV has built its business by acquiring, developing, and scaling electric bicycle and micro-mobility brands. While Rad Power is perhaps its highest-profile purchase, the company also holds an equity interest in LEV Manufacturing, Inc., which acquired the Serial 1 premium electric bicycle brand originally developed and spun off from Harley-Davidson.

In a statement, Life EV said the acquisition fits into its broader strategy of expansion across North America.

Title: Grading the Islanders’ Trade for the Blues’ Brayden Schenn
Author: Alex Chauvancy
Summary: The New York Islanders traded for Brayden Schenn from the St. Louis Blues, giving up a first-round pick, Jonathan Drouin, and a goaltending prospect. Schenn, with declining performance and a $6.5 million cap hit through next season, may not significantly improve the team. The Blues received a strong return, while the Islanders' move is viewed as questionable.
Content:
The New York Islanders have had an interesting few weeks ahead of the NHL trade deadline. Shortly before the Olympic break, they acquired Ondřej Palát and a couple of draft picks from the New Jersey Devils in exchange for Maxim Tsyplakov. Today, they made one of the splashier deals of Deadline Day, acquiring Brayden Schenn from the St. Louis Blues. 

Here are the full details:

Isles acquire: Schenn  
Blues acquire: 2026 1st-round pick, 3rd-round pick, Jonathan Drouin, goaltending prospect Marcus Gildon

Schenn had to waive his no-trade clause to join the Islanders, but it may have been better for the Islanders had he invoked it. This move is a head-scratcher that doesn’t make much sense for where they are as an organization. As for the Blues, they made out like bandits and got full value for their longtime captain.

The Islanders have been a surprise this season and look like a safe bet to make the playoffs at this point. That’s why GM Mathieu Darche decided to become a buyer, but it’s questionable whether his moves have made the team significantly better. Palát’s best days seem behind him, and the same appears true for Schenn.

Schenn has 12 goals and 28 points in 61 games this season, which is decent production, but there are red flags. His skating has declined in recent seasons, which is concerning given the Islanders’ uptempo style. He also struggles in transition. His shooting ability has also fallen off in the last couple of seasons. While he still scores at a decent clip, his volume has decreased. He is a good playmaker and excels in faceoffs, which likely contributed to the Islanders’ decision to acquire him.

Still, there are more cons than pros in this deal. One issue is Schenn’s contract, which runs through next season at a cap hit of $6.5 million. This trade would have been more palatable if he were a rental, but the extra year complicates matters. Additionally, he makes the Islanders a slower team, which Palát already did.

The Islanders also gave up quite a bit to acquire Schenn. Drouin struggled with the Islanders, and while they aren’t losing much there, giving up a first-round pick for a declining player in his mid-30s with term is questionable asset management. It remains to be seen if this trade will work out as intended.

**Islanders Grade: C**  
**Blues maximized Schenn’s value**  

The Blues had another trade nixed by a no-trade clause when Colton Parayko did not waive to join the Sabres. This trade, however, went through, allowing them to get a solid return for their longtime captain. Drouin did struggle with the Islanders but has been productive at times over the last few seasons.

Title: Weekly Chartstopper: March 6, 2026
Author: Michael Normyle
Summary: Since the Iran conflict began, oil prices have surged by over 30%, impacting U.S. inflation and GDP growth. Markets have lowered expectations for Fed rate cuts and seen a slight Nasdaq decline. The 10-year Treasury yield rose to 4.15%. Key upcoming data include CPI, PCE, GDP revisions, and job reports.
Content:
US Markets Weekly Chartstopper: March 6, 2026

March 06, 2026 — 05:00 pm EST

Since the conflict with Iran began on Saturday, markets have been trying to assess its economic impact, primarily through higher energy prices. The conflict has effectively halted traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, which transports 20-25% of global oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG), and has shuttered energy facilities in the region.

This week, international and U.S. oil prices have increased by 30% and 35%, respectively, reaching their highest levels since 2023. European LNG prices are up 65%, while U.S. LNG prices have risen by 10%. JPMorgan estimates that if oil prices remain at these levels, U.S. headline inflation could increase by 0.3 percentage points, and U.S. GDP growth could decrease by 0.6 percentage points. 

Given this likely boost to inflation, markets have reduced their expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts this year by 20 basis points to around 40 basis points. This adjustment follows a significant jobs report miss, with the economy losing 92,000 jobs in February—against expectations of a 55,000 gain—and broad-based sector losses. However, it’s important not to overinterpret a single month’s data, as the private sector has averaged a gain of about 20,000 jobs per month over the past three months.

Despite these developments, the Nasdaq-100® is down just 1% this week, though rising inflation expectations have pushed the 10-year Treasury yield up nearly 20 basis points to 4.15%.

Next Week

Here are the top events to watch:

- Tuesday: NFIB Small Business Optimism (February)
- Wednesday: CPI Inflation (February)
- Thursday: Jobless Claims
- Friday: PCE Inflation and Spending (January), Real GDP (Q4 revision), JOLTS Job Openings (January)

Michael Normyle  
U.S. Economist and Senior Director at Nasdaq

Title: Avalanche Acquire Nazem Kadri From Calgary Flames
Author: Matteo Giuliano
Summary: Nazem Kadri was traded from the Calgary Flames to the Colorado Avalanche for a 2028 first-round pick, a 2027 second-round pick, Victor Olofsson, and Max Curran, with Calgary retaining 20% of Kadri’s $7 million salary. The Avalanche strengthen their lineup, boosting Stanley Cup hopes, while the Flames acquire multiple draft picks and prospects for rebuilding.
Content:
We have potentially the trade of the deadline, with Nazem Kadri and a 2027 fourth-round pick being traded to the Colorado Avalanche. The Calgary Flames will receive a 2028 first-round pick, a 2027 second-round pick, Victor Olofsson, and Max Curran. The Flames will retain 20 percent of Kadri’s contract, which is $1.4 million, as part of the deal.

What the Avalanche Are Getting

Avalanche fans are already familiar with him, but Kadri is the ideal third-line centre for any team. He is a physical presence in a lineup and has found a way to score goals on one of the lowest-scoring teams in the NHL. While his stats this season haven’t been fantastic, with just 10 goals and 29 assists in 59 games, he scored 35 goals just last season for the Flames.

Trade talks around Kadri have been ongoing since his first year in Calgary, where everything went wrong for the Flames. The Jonathan Huberdeau trade happened in the same offseason as the Kadri signing, and nothing gelled. That trade is now regarded by many as one of the worst trades in NHL history.

Related: Panthers are Winning the Matthew Tkachuk Trade

Kadri is a fantastic player, but his age and contract are concerns for the future. He is already 35 and is under contract for another three seasons after this one, with an average annual value of $7 million. Even with the salary cap increasing, it is a pricey deal; however, if they win the cup with Kadri, none of this will matter.

New Stanley Cup Favorite?

The biggest weakness with the Avalanche lineup was the center position heading into the deadline. After the deadline, Colorado acquired a new third- and fourth-line center. A lineup of Nathan MacKinnon, Brock Nelson, Nazem Kadri, and Nicolas Roy provides elite depth. With a lineup that was very top-heavy before the deadline, that no longer appears to be the case.

With the league being so wide open this season, Colorado is now a favorite to win their second Stanley Cup in five years.

The Flames Continue Their Rebuild

The Flames receive a first- and a second-round pick as part of the deal. They now have six first-round picks in the next three seasons. It is unlikely they will use all six selections in the first round over that period, which means these picks could be used as assets for future deals. They also received prospect Max Curran, who has posted 14 goals and 27 assists for a total of 41 points in 31 games with the Edmonton Oil Kings.

Flames general manager Craig Conroy did well to acquire assets in this deal, especially considering concerns over Kadri’s contract length, price, and his age of 35.

Title: Best Xbox Game Pass games to play this weekend (March 6-8 2026)
Author: Austin Manchester
Summary: March's new releases, including Pokémon Pokopia and Slay the Spire 2, are not on Xbox Game Pass. However, the service offers popular titles like Resident Evil Requiem, now the fastest-selling in the series, and the Resident Evil 2 remake. Other recommended Game Pass games include Planet of Lana 2 and the original Slay the Spire, a key roguelike deckbuilder.
Content:
While March is off to a strong start for new games already, with Pokémon Pokopia, Slay the Spire 2, and Marathonall releasing this past week, none of those are on Xbox Game Pass. Neither is February's Resident Evil Requiem, one of the year's best games so far and one that might end up in the Game of the Year conversation by year's end. But you know what is on Game Pass? A bounty of excellent games well worth checking out.

This week's recommendations include a precursor to Requiem, an indie hit, and a must-play roguelike deckbuilder.

Everyone’s talking about Resident Evil Requiem right now, partly because it’s become the fastest-selling game in the series’s history, and mostly because grown-up Leon looks incredibly hot. Whether you’ve already finished Requiem or you’re holding out for a sale, now’s the perfect time to revisit the stellar Resident Evil 2 remake on Game Pass. As rookie cop Leon, you’re tasked with escaping Raccoon City police headquarters and uncovering the nefarious secrets of the Umbrella Corporation. RE2 is a thrilling, exquisitely paced adventure that you can easily devour in a weekend. And if you’re left wanting more, you can double your pleasure by playing Claire Redfield’s route.

Planet of Lana 2: Children of the Leaf is a stylish and cinematic indie puzzle-platformer. Without recognizable language, it tells a deeply personal narrative through expressive animation, sweeping music, and standout vocal performances that sound like gibberish to our ears. The mechanics are deeper than the first game's, with new ways to tackle puzzles, such as using Mui's ability to hypnotize creatures to progress. Planet of Lana 2 opens with a recap of the first game, so you don’t technically need to have played the 2023 hidden gem to enjoy its sequel. But both games are available on Game Pass.

Slay the Spire 2 just had one of the biggest game launches of the year. But if you’re curious to hear what all the hype is about before jumping in, the first game is still available. Mega Crit's 2019 roguelike deckbuilder is arguably the game that kicked off the current roguelike deckbuilding trend. Its mix of cards and characters is an immediate draw for strategy enthusiasts. The unique visuals and the depth of run variety keep players coming back for years. Slay the Spire 2 builds on the original's fundamentals, but if you want a taste of what to expect, the first game offers a solid introduction.

Title: U.S. says it worked with Ecuador to bomb alleged drug-trafficking operations site
Author: Unknown
Summary: The U.S. and Ecuador conducted a joint operation targeting a Colombian FARC dissident drug training camp near Ecuador's border, using helicopters, aircraft, boats, and drones. Details on casualties are undisclosed. Ecuador's President Noboa emphasizes a crackdown on organized crime and values U.S. support. The operation aligns with regional efforts to combat drug trafficking and narco-terrorism.
Content:
The U.S. and Ecuador carried out a joint operation targeting drug trafficking in Ecuador, authorities in both countries announced on Friday. The U.S. described the operation as "lethal kinetic operations." Neither U.S. Southern Command nor Ecuador's defense ministry provided details on whether anyone was killed or captured during the strike.

Ecuador's defense ministry stated that the operation involved helicopters, aircraft, river boats, and drones to locate and bomb a drug traffickers' training camp in northeastern Ecuador near the Colombian border. The camp belonged to the Comandos de la Frontera (CDF), a Colombian criminal organization composed of FARC dissidents, with a capacity for 50 people.

Ecuadorian President Daniel Noboa has prioritized a military crackdown on organized crime. His government has also imposed tariffs on Colombia, accusing it of insufficient efforts to combat drug trafficking. Noboa is scheduled to travel to Miami this weekend to participate in the Trump administration's "Shield of the Americas" summit, which gathers regional leaders to discuss security and organized crime.

The Ecuadorian defense ministry stated, "The United States is a key ally in this fight," and noted that the operation was conducted at Ecuador's request to dismantle narco-terrorist networks. This operation followed a similar joint effort announced earlier this week by U.S. Southern Command.

Title: Resident Evil Requiem is a timely critique of capitalist greed
Author: Josh Broadwell
Summary: Resident Evil Requiem offers a deeper critique of capitalism, highlighting how elites like Victor Gideon manipulate blood and memories to create clones and control individuals. It depicts experiments at the Raccoon City Orphanage and the exploitation of vulnerable people, symbolizing broader themes of societal manipulation, power, and greed, with a focus on the corrupting influence of those in control.
Content:
As much as Resident Evil changed over the years, a few things remained constant with the series' attitude toward society. Corporations are bad, rich people are even worse, and corporate scientists are also bad. The themes usually don't go much further than that. But Resident Evil Requiem has a more carefully thought-out critique of capitalism and selfishness more broadly, and it's a timely one as well. 

Victor Gideon, one of Requiem's primary antagonists, is the focal point of Capcom's critique. He wants to realize Oswell Spencer's presumed goal of preserving the consciousness of a "superior" person by transplanting it into another host to create new generations of "elites," similar to real-life tech and business moguls. It is revealed that the Raccoon City Orphanage was a front for these experiments, with dozens of clone children created for this purpose. Central to Gideon’s plans is the idea that people's memories and essences are stored in blood, hence all the blood-related work he's doing at the Rhodes Care Home. This concept isn't just Resident Evil hokum; the idea that blood contains part of the human spirit and could transfer it between hosts persisted for centuries, dating back to the Middle Ages.

The ideal result is a person who remains an individual but is inclined to think like Spencer, know what he knows, and behave like him, if Gideon’s words about Grace are any indication. The operation resembles a gruesome algorithm that takes pieces from everyone's experiences and inserts a predetermined bias to produce a result that benefits the creator more than anyone else. This can be seen as a metaphor for social media, where a handful of malicious individuals manipulate perceptions and beliefs.

Connections become even more explicit in the care center’s basement, where Gideon and his team perform gruesome experiments, including using a mulcher to extract blood for their work. If the blood theory they operate on is correct, they are not just taking lives—they are harvesting the essence of existence itself: memories, souls, everything that makes a person alive. They use this for their own ends, treating human life as a plaything. 

"The little people" have always suffered through no fault of their own in Resident Evil, but Requiem emphasizes how grotesque it is when someone wields such power over others’ lives—using vulnerable individuals who believe they are receiving help, only to turn them into monsters when it suits the oppressors. 

Requiem's storytelling takes a long pause once Leon Kennedy reaches Raccoon City, but the critique of exploitation and the corrupting influence of power remains central to its narrative.

Title: How to get bricks in Pokémon Pokopia
Author: Josh Broadwell
Summary: In Pokémon Pokopia, bricks are needed for advanced building projects, obtained by processing squishy clay with Burn Pokémon like Charmander or Torchic. Clay is collected after certain in-game events, such as rain-softened Onix or Wooper habitats. Players can process up to 10 lumps of clay into bricks daily, with guides available for island upgrades, story requests, and multiplayer features.
Content:
This article is part of a directory: Pokémon Pokopia guides and walkthroughs. 

You'll need bricks in Pokémon Pokopia to complete more advanced, non-leaf building projects, though the game doesn't specify how to obtain bricks early on. You'll need a specific resource and the help of a Pokémon with the Burn specialty, such as Charmander, Torchic, Litwick, Growlithe, or Vulpix. Since Charmander is available in the first area and Torchic in Bleak Beach, you'll be able to make bricks as soon as you acquire clay.

With clay in hand, speak to your Burn Pokémon of choice, select "Look at this!", show them the clay, and ask them to turn it into bricks. One lump of squishy clay yields two bricks, and you can process up to 10 lumps at a time. It takes a few minutes for the Pokémon to fire the clay into bricks, but there is no limit on how many times per day you can have them do this.

Bricks are not typically found in the wild like rocks. To get squishy clay, you can start harvesting after completing the "Yawn up a Storm" request, when rain softens the clay trapping Onix. Use Rock Smash to break the blocks and collect the squishy clay left behind. There are several clay blocks in the cave system and more in the Bleak Beach region. Additionally, if you create a habitat for Paldean Wooper, you may find squishy clay around that habitat, as Wooper drops it periodically due to its Litter specialty.

For new players, guides are available to help you upgrade your island, including a Pokédex and Habitat Dex to identify spawn locations and necessary building materials. There are also walkthroughs for key story requests like "Brighten Things Up!", "Time to Party!", and "Build the Huge Building!". Multiplayer options are available but with certain restrictions for regular Pokopia multiplayer and the Game Share version.

Title: Nintendo sues the US government for a refund on tariffs
Author: Amanda Silberling
Summary: Nintendo filed a lawsuit in the U.S. Court of International Trade seeking a refund for tariffs imposed under President Trump's executive orders, which were struck down by the Supreme Court for exceeding presidential authority. Over a thousand companies are also suing for refunds on tariffs totaling over $200 billion. Trump raised tariffs from 10% to 15%, prompting additional legal challenges.
Content:
Nintendo filed a lawsuit against the U.S. government on Friday seeking a refund for tariffs paid due to President Donald Trump's executive orders invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The lawsuit was filed in the U.S. Court of International Trade after a Supreme Court decision struck down the tariffs imposed under IEEPA, ruling that the president exceeded his authority. More than a thousand other companies have also sued for refunds on these tariffs, which have resulted in over $200 billion collected on imports.

Nintendo confirmed the filing, stating, "We have nothing else to share on the topic." In response to the Supreme Court’s decision, President Trump raised tariffs from 10% to 15%. Currently, 24 states have sued, arguing that the president has overstepped his powers by making this change.

Title: X is testing a new ad format that connects posts with products
Author: Sarah Perez
Summary: X is testing a new ad format that inserts product recommendations beneath posts referencing the company or its products, initially spotted in Europe with Starlink. The feature, confirmed by X's product head, aims to provide non-intrusive suggestions. This follows earlier initiatives like “Paid Partnership” labels and efforts to attract creators, enhancing monetization and competitiveness against platforms like Instagram and TikTok.
Content:
X is testing a new ad format that inserts a recommendation directly underneath a post that references the company or its products. The initial test, spotted by an X user in Europe, displayed a suggestion to “Get Starlink” beneath a post from a user that said Starlink’s satellite service works great in Portugal. The link, when clicked, directed users to Starlink’s website. X head of product Nikita Bier confirmed the test, responding, “Trying to make an ad product that isn’t an ad.”

The Starlink ad is not visible to all users at this time, but the placeholder where the ad sits is. If a user posts from @levelsio on March 6, there will be an outlined box beneath the text of the post. This box currently showcases a random X post unless the user is in a location where the ad test is live. 

In places where the ad displays, several commenters noticed the new addition, with one asking, “lmao, did you add this Starlink button?” In response to a suggestion that X should allow affiliate links in this ad slot, Bier said, “No, then people will lie. I want to trust recommendations on here.” 

The test follows earlier news that the company is rolling out “Paid Partnership” labels for creators. These labels can be applied to posts to comply with social media advertising regulations, instead of requiring creators to use hashtags like “ad” or “paid partnership.” If sponsored posts were combined with embedded links like the one being tested, X could potentially attract more marketers to the platform. This could encourage creators to use the app more, helping it compete against larger social networks such as Instagram, YouTube, and TikTok.

X has been pursuing creator content for some time—long before it was called “X” and before Elon Musk’s ownership. The platform has introduced various creator products, including payouts for viral content, ad-revenue sharing, and creator subscriptions. Recently, the company revamped its Creator Subscriptions offering with new features, including the ability to monetize individual threads.

Additionally, X announced that its integrated chatbot Grok can now read X’s long-form content, known as Articles. However, this feature remains underutilized, as creators who publish lengthy texts often prefer their own websites or newsletters.

Title: CyHi Addresses G.O.O.D. Music Breakup on "The G.O.O.D. Ole Days"
Author: Trent Fitzgerald
Summary: Cy Hi addresses the breakup of G.O.O.D. Music in his song "The G.O.O.D. Ole Days," blaming strained relationships with Travis Scott and Kid Cudi over issues like co-writing "Sicko Mode" and siding with Kanye West. He also discusses Kanye's role in the group's dissolution and announces a new album, "The Story of Mr. EGOT."
Content:
Cy Hi addresses Travis Scott, Kid Cudi, and the reasons behind the G.O.O.D. Music breakup in his new song, "The G.O.O.D. Ole Days." On Friday (March 6), Cy Hi released the song, produced by Mark Byrd and Phil Blackman, where he discusses artists from Ye's G.O.O.D. Music label and why the breakup happened.

In the song, the Georgia rapper explains his fallout with G.O.O.D. Music members Travis Scott and Kid Cudi. Cy Hi claims that his friendship with Travis deteriorated when he couldn't get a verse from him in exchange for co-writing Travis's biggest hit, "Sicko Mode." He raps, "I like what he did in fashion, but ni**a just look hella tacky. When I helped him with his album, it went like [11-times] platinum, but I still ain't got my swap and he mad that I'm even askin'."

Regarding Kid Cudi, Cy Hi believes their relationship soured because he sided with Ye during a past disagreement with Cudi. He raps, "It could have got real ugly / They said that Ye almost f**k around and killed buddy, and that's why their relationship is still muddy. And I'm caught between it all. Man, ni**a should feel for me."

As for Ye, Cy Hi acknowledges that Ye was also a reason for G.O.O.D. Music's dissolution but empathizes with him, noting that Ye was trying to keep the group together. He lyrically explains, "Okay, I understand that. But you know sometimes Kanye could be a little difficult. I get it, but I understand him in a different way. Y'all just know the beautiful dark twisty Ye. He's just an honest person, but it gets conveyed as somebody with a broken heart against the 808. I ain't going to lie. Sometimes it gets in the business way. He just ain't afraid to say what you didn't say."

Near the end of the song, Cy Hi announces he's dropping a new album titled "The Story of Mr. EGOT" "real soon."

Title: Buy the Spike in CrowdStrike or Okta Stock After Posting Record Profitability?
Author: Zacks
Summary: CrowdStrike and Okta outperformed expectations with record Q4 profits, strong revenue growth, and expanding recurring revenues. CrowdStrike’s $1.3 billion sales, driven by AI-driven cybersecurity demand, and Okta’s $761 million sales, boosted by enterprise adoption, led to stock surges of over 10%. Both companies reported record profitability and positive full-year guidance amid heightened cybersecurity needs.
Content:
Technology

Buy the Spike in CrowdStrike or Okta Stock After Posting Record Profitability?

In a week marked by broader market volatility, cybersecurity firms CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Okta (OKTA) have emerged as outperformers after exceeding their Q4 expectations. Both stocks have surged more than 10% since reporting their Q4 results on Tuesday and Wednesday, respectively.

Both companies delivered standout Q4 reports, showing record profitability, strong revenue growth, expanding recurring revenue bases, and rising demand for cybersecurity and identity solutions.

CrowdStrike’s AI-Driven Security Demand

CrowdStrike’s strong Q1 results were defined by faster-than-expected growth amid surging demand for its Falcon cybersecurity platform as AI-driven cyber threats become more sophisticated. Notably, Falcon is a cloud-native cybersecurity solution that provides advanced endpoint detection and response (EDR), managed detection and response (MDR), and next-generation antivirus capabilities to protect organizations from cyber threats.

Falcon’s growth led to CrowdStrike posting Q4 sales of $1.3 billion, up 24% from a year ago and exceeding estimates of $1.29 billion. Its net new annual recurring revenue (ARR) grew 47% year-over-year, reaching a quarterly record of $331 million. More importantly, CrowdStrike saw record profitability since going public in 2019, with Q4 EPS rising 9% to $1.12, surpassing expectations of $1.10.

Okta’s Enterprise Adoption & Platform Consolidation

Okta’s standout Q4 report was highlighted by steady subscription growth, expanding enterprise adoption, and strong cash flow, signaling a successful transition for its now unified identity security platform. The consolidated platform allows customers to integrate with nearly any application, service, or cloud through a secure, reliable, and scalable security system. Accelerating adoption of Okta's platform was attributed to trust from large organizations as they modernize their security architecture.

Solid revenue growth saw Okta’s Q4 sales increase 11% to $761 million, beating estimates of $749.1 million. Its remaining performance obligations (RPO) rose 15% year-over-year, with current RPO growing 12%, indicating strong future revenue. Okta also posted record profitability, with Q4 EPS up 15% to $0.90, beating expectations of $0.85. Other highlights included Q4 free cash flow rising 11% to a record $252 million.

Full-Year Results & Guidance

Capping off a record year, CrowdStrike’s annual sales surged 22% to $4.81 billion, with full-year EPS increasing significantly from previous levels.

Title: Par Petroleum (PARR) Q4 Earnings: How Key Metrics Compare to Wall Street Estimates
Author: Zacks
Summary: Par Petroleum reported Q4 revenue of $1.81 billion, slightly below estimates but with a 1% YoY decline. EPS was $1.17, beating revenue expectations but missing EPS estimates. Key refinery throughput metrics exceeded estimates, and gross margin at Washington was higher than expected. Revenue from refining surpassed estimates, while retail and logistics revenues were close to projections.
Content:
Technology Par Petroleum (PARR) Q4 Earnings: How Key Metrics Compare to Wall Street Estimates

For the quarter ended December 2025, Par Petroleum (PARR) reported revenue of $1.81 billion, down 1% over the same period last year. EPS came in at $1.17, compared to -$0.79 in the year-ago quarter. The reported revenue represents a surprise of +5.9% over the Zacks Consensus Estimate of $1.71 billion. With the consensus EPS estimate being $1.21, the EPS surprise was -2.91%.

While investors closely watch year-over-year changes in headline numbers—revenue and earnings—and how they compare to Wall Street expectations to determine their next course of action, some key metrics always provide a better insight into a company's underlying performance. Since these metrics play a crucial role in driving the top- and bottom-line numbers, comparing them with the year-ago numbers and what analysts estimated helps investors better project a stock's price performance.

Here is how Par Petroleum performed in the just reported quarter in terms of the metrics most widely monitored and projected by Wall Street analysts:

- Total Refining - Feedstocks Throughput: 190,900 thousand barrels of oil versus the three-analyst average estimate of 189,129.2 thousand barrels.
- Hawaii Refinery - Feedstocks Throughput: 87.10 million barrels per day (MMBBL/D) compared to the 85.96 MMBBL/D average estimate.
- Washington Refinery - Feedstocks Throughput: 37.00 MMBBL/D versus 36.08 MMBBL/D estimated by three analysts.
- Wyoming Refinery - Feedstocks Throughput: 14.40 MMBBL/D compared to the 15.53 MMBBL/D average estimate.
- Montana Refinery - Feedstocks Throughput: 52.40 MMBBL/D compared to the 51.55 MMBBL/D estimate.

- Adjusted Gross Margin per barrel at Washington Refinery: $8.32 versus $6.39 estimated by two analysts.
- Production costs per barrel at Wyoming Refinery: $13.27 versus the two-analyst average estimate of $10.25.
- Production costs per barrel at Washington Refinery: $4.57 compared to the $4.08 estimate.
- Production costs per barrel at Hawaii Refinery: $4.15 versus the two-analyst average estimate of $4.70.

- Revenues from Refining: $1.75 billion versus the two-analyst estimate of $1.57 billion. This represents a year-over-year change of -1.2%.
- Revenues from Retail: $142.28 million compared to the $145.87 million estimate, a +0.5% change year over year.
- Revenues from Logistics: $73.71 million versus the $74 million estimate.

Title: Pokémon Go 'Pokémon 30th Anniversary: All Out' event guide
Author: Julia Lee
Summary: Pokémon Go's "Pokémon 30th Anniversary" event runs from March 3-9, featuring wild Kanto Pokémon, special research, and raid bosses. An "All Out" upgrade begins March 7, adding legendary raids, regional Ultra Beasts, and boosted bonuses. Baby Pokémon hatch from 2 km eggs, with various Pokémon appearing in the wild and in raids, excluding Mewtwo, Ultra Beasts, and certain mythicals.
Content:
Pokémon Go's "Pokémon 30th Anniversary" event is here, celebrating the Kanto region once again. The event runs from March 3-9, with an upgrade to an "All Out" version beginning on March 7, which changes spawns and bonuses for the weekend.

During the event, all Kanto region Pokémon, including Articuno, Zapdos, and Moltres, can be found in the wild, except Mewtwo and Mew. A special Go Pass is available, rewarding encounters with various Kanto Pokémon and a free Mewtwo encounter at rank 80. Milestone rewards include doubled catch candy at rank 25 and doubled catch XP at rank 50, with these bonuses tripled if the premium pass is purchased.

Ditto disguises have been updated, though the new forms are still being identified. The event features Field Research tasks, boosted spawns, and raid bosses.

Starting March 7 at 10 a.m. until March 9 at 8 p.m., the event is upgraded to the "All Out" version, offering additional perks. Incensed used during this period may lead to legendary Pokémon encounters if luck is on your side. Pikachu wearing trainer-themed hats will appear in one-star raids. Five-star raids will feature Articuno, Zapdos, Moltres, Deoxys (Normal Form), Darkrai, Genesect (No Drive), Nihilego, Buzzwole, Pheromosa, Xurkitree, Celesteela, Kartana, Guzzlord, Stakataka, Blacephalon, and Necrozma. Regional Ultra Beasts will only appear in their respective regions: Buzzwole in the Americas and Greenland, Pheromosa in Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and India, and Xurkitree in Asia-Pacific.

Baby Pokémon such as Pichu, Cleffa, Igglybuff, Togepi, Tyrogue, Smoochum, Elekid, Magby, Azurill, Wynaut, Budew, Chingling, Bonsly, Mime Jr., Happiny, Munchlax, Riolu, Mantyke, and Toxel will hatch from 2 km eggs. Mime Jr. remains region-locked to Europe.

All Pokémon released so far will be available in the wild, excluding baby Pokémon, costumed Pokémon, Ultra Beasts, some mythical Pokémon, and others like Mewtwo, Smeargle, Shedinja, Kecleon, Giratina, Zoroark, Larvesta, Volcarona, Scatterbug, Spewpa, Vivillon, Zygarde, Mudbray, Mudsdale, Cosmog, Cosmoem, Morpeko, Eternatus, Kubfu, Urshifu, Maushold, Dudunsparce, Gimmighoul, and Gholdengo.

Title: Ducks Trade Ryan Strome to Flames for 7th-Round Pick
Author: Owen Hillman
Summary: The Anaheim Ducks traded forward Ryan Strome to the Calgary Flames for a 2027 seventh-round pick, gaining cap relief. Strome, signed for $25 million in 2022, struggled this season due to injuries and declining production. The Flames, rebuilding, seek veteran leadership, while Anaheim benefits from cap space and future draft assets.
Content:
The Anaheim Ducks have traded forward Ryan Strome to the Calgary Flames for a 2027 seventh-round pick. The Ducks received some cap relief and moved off a contract, while the Flames acquired a veteran to mentor potential young players.

The Ducks signed Strome to a five-year, $25 million contract on July 13, 2022. The deal, along with contracts for Frank Vatrano, Alex Killorn, and Radko Gudas, was intended to establish a team culture. With promising forwards like Mason McTavish, Leo Carlsson, and Cutter Gauthier, Strome was expected to serve as a mentor.

He was a decent contributor, averaging 41 points between the 2022-23 and 2024-25 seasons. However, his production declined sharply this season, with just three goals and three assists in 33 games. Part of this was due to an upper-body injury in the preseason that sidelined him for 16 games, along with illnesses in March.

Despite his struggles, he remains under contract at $5 million per season until 2027. With Carlsson and McTavish developing into franchise players, and the team signing Mikael Granlund to a three-year, $21 million deal in 2025, Strome’s role was diminishing. Anaheim was only able to secure a seventh-round pick for him, but the trade provided some cap relief, as the Ducks have over $49 million in cap space this season and need to address pending restricted free agents.

The Flames, in rebuild mode, sought veteran leadership through this deal. They did not sell off all assets at the deadline, with Blake Coleman remaining on the roster. With 55 points, they are positioned to contend for a top draft pick in the 2026 NHL Draft, holding the second-highest lottery odds behind the Vancouver Canucks.

Strome could benefit from an elevated role with the Flames, offering positional flexibility at wing and center. He still possesses playmaking abilities and could assist young players like Matthew Coronato and Connor Zary. Calgary didn’t give up much to acquire him and has significant draft capital, including two first-round picks and four second-round picks.

Title: Weekly Chartstopper: February 27, 2026
Author: Michael Normyle
Summary: AI fears caused market volatility, with a viral blog post triggering a selloff. Positive news from Anthropic boosted software stocks, while NVIDIA's strong earnings highlighted AI chip demand despite a stock dip. Investors sought safety amid geopolitical tensions, lowering Treasury yields and leaving the Nasdaq-100 flat. Upcoming events include manufacturing data, earnings, productivity, and payroll reports.
Content:
Markets Weekly Chartstopper: February 27, 2026

February 27, 2026 — 04:50 pm EST

Artificial intelligence (AI) fears may have reached new heights this week, with some attributing Monday’s selloff to a futuristic viral blog post. But, in the here and now, there was some more positive AI news: Anthropic’s update on its enterprise AI agents actually boosted some software stocks since it includes plugins for some of the very same software companies caught up in the months-long AI-related selloff.

NVIDIA’s fourth-quarter earnings highlighted continued AI chip demand, nearly doubling profits year on year and projecting higher first-quarter revenue ($78 billion) than expected. And yet, with investors questioning the durability of demand, the stock was down over 5% the next day.

So, between concerns about AI’s economic impact and geopolitical tensions, there’s been a flight to safety this week, pushing down 10-year Treasury yields over 10 basis points to approximately 3.95%, while the Nasdaq-100® ended the week flat.

Next Week

Here are the top events I’m watching next week:
- Monday: Manufacturing PMIs (Feb.)
- Wednesday: AVGO earnings (Q4) & Services PMIs (Feb.)
- Thursday: Productivity (Q4)
- Friday: Nonfarm payrolls (Feb.) & Retail sales (Jan.)

Title: Texans grant Mixon release after lost '25 season
Author: DJ Bien-Aime
Summary: The Texans released veteran RB Joe Mixon after he missed the 2025 season due to a foot injury. Mixon requested his release, which was granted, saving $8 million in cap space. He had rushed for 1,016 yards and 11 TDs in 2024, earning a Pro Bowl. Houston replaced him with David Montgomery to improve their rushing attack.
Content:
The Texans released veteran running back Joe Mixon on Friday after he missed the 2025 season with a foot injury. Mixon requested his release Thursday, a source told ESPN's Adam Schefter. A day later, the Texans granted it, releasing him with a non-football injury designation. The move saves Houston $8 million in cap space.

Mixon, 29, suffered a mysterious foot injury during the offseason, the specifics of which the Texans never disclosed. As a result, Mixon missed all the team's offseason workouts and training camp. Houston then placed Mixon on the non-football injury list at the end of training camp, which automatically sidelined him for four games. Although there was hope he could come back, Mixon's foot never healed. Houston leaned on rookie Woody Marks, who had a team-high 703 rushing yards, and free agent signing Nick Chubb, who had 506 yards.

Houston finished just 22nd in the league in rushing yards per game (108). To improve in that area, the Texans agreed to a deal to acquire David Montgomery from the Detroit Lions earlier this week.

In 2024, Mixon rushed for 1,016 yards and 11 touchdowns, earning him Pro Bowl honors in his first season with the Texans. He had 159 yards and a touchdown in his first game in Houston against the Indianapolis Colts. He suffered an ankle injury that year but returned to post six more 100-yard rushing outings during the regular season. Mixon also rushed for 194 yards and two touchdowns during the Texans' 2024 playoff run, which ended with a loss to the Kansas City Chiefs in the divisional round.

Houston acquired Mixon via trade from the Cincinnati Bengals in March 2024 after he was expected to be released. The franchise immediately signed him to a three-year, $27 million contract extension, including $13 million guaranteed.

Title: Source: Patriots trading center Bradbury to Bears
Author: Courtney Cronin
Summary: Drew Dalman, age 27, retires from the NFL, prompting the Bears to acquire center Garrett Bradbury from the Patriots in a trade involving a 2027 fifth-round pick. Bradbury, a former first-round pick, started all games last season and provides stability to Chicago's offensive line. The Bears have created $44 million in cap space through recent moves.
Content:
Drew Dalman is retiring from the NFL at age 27, leaving the Chicago Bears to address the center position again. The Bears agreed in principle to send a 2027 fifth-round pick to the New England Patriots for center Garrett Bradbury, with the trade to be processed once the new league year begins next Wednesday.

Bradbury, a 2019 first-round pick, spent his first six seasons with the Minnesota Vikings before signing a two-year deal worth up to $12 million with New England in March 2025. He started all 17 regular-season games and all four playoff games in his lone season with the Patriots. His veteran presence helped stabilize the offensive line, which started rookies in key positions.

The Bears will owe Bradbury $4.7 million in the final year of his contract. His 105 career starts provide stability to the Bears' offensive line, which has lost 40% of its starters this offseason due to Dalman's retirement and injuries to other players. Dalman was officially placed on the reserve/retired list Friday, while the Bears terminated the contract of linebacker Tremaine Edmunds. Along with wide receiver DJ Moore being traded to the Buffalo Bills, Chicago has created $44 million in cap space since Tuesday.

Bradbury's most productive seasons came during his early years in Minnesota in a zone scheme offense that featured play-action and misdirection. Under Bears coach Ben Johnson, Chicago used under-center formations 49% of the time in 2025 and ran play-action on 32% of dropbacks. The team also ranked sixth in the NFL for explosive plays at 15.0%.

For New England, Wilson, a 2025 third-round pick, is likely to replace Bradbury. Wilson played center at the University of Georgia but was moved to left guard as part of the team's approach of playing its best five linemen regardless of position.

Title: Sources: Ravens to acquire Crosby in blockbuster
Author: Jamison Hensley
Summary: The Baltimore Ravens acquired five-time Pro Bowl DE Maxx Crosby from the Las Vegas Raiders for first-round picks in 2026 and 2027, marking their first trade of a veteran for a first-rounder. Crosby, 28, boosts a weak pass rush and aims to help the Ravens reach the Super Bowl. The deal is pending a physical and follows a disappointing 2025 season.
Content:
In the biggest trade in franchise history, the Baltimore Ravens are acquiring five-time Pro Bowl defensive end Maxx Crosby from the Las Vegas Raiders for first-round picks in 2026 and 2027, sources told ESPN's Adam Schefter on Friday night. The deal is contingent on Crosby passing a physical, which can't occur until the start of the new league year Wednesday.

This marks the first time in the Ravens' 31-year existence that they've used a first-round pick to trade for a veteran player. Baltimore had the No. 14 pick in this year's draft. Among the teams the Ravens beat out for Crosby were the Dallas Cowboys, who were willing to offer a first- and a second-round pick for the star pass rusher.

Crosby, 28, significantly upgrades a struggling Ravens pass rush that in 2025 totaled 30 sacks, Baltimore's fewest in 15 years. Last season was also the first time since the Ravens' inaugural 1996 campaign that they didn't have an edge rusher with more than 4.5 sacks.

The Ravens are making this uncharacteristic offseason splash after a disappointing 2025 season in which they finished 8-9, failed to make the playoffs, and then fired longtime coach John Harbaugh. Baltimore has been looking for a game changer, such as Crosby, to get over the postseason hurdle and reach the Super Bowl for the first time since the 2012 season.

The addition of Crosby gives the Ravens their most decorated and feared pass rusher in his prime since Terrell Suggs, the franchise's all-time sacks leader, who played in Baltimore from 2003 to 2018. Crosby has produced four double-digit sack seasons in his seven-year career. Over that span, the Ravens have had two edge rushers with 10 or more sacks in a season: Kyle Van Noy (12.5 in 2024) and Odafe Oweh (10 in 2024).

This is a huge addition for new Ravens coach Jesse Minter, who will call plays on defense. Since entering the league in 2019, Crosby has 360 quarterback pressures, which is 29 more than the next-closest player, according to ESPN Research.

The Ravens have been reluctant to give up coveted draft capital because of their philosophy of building through the draft. Before Baltimore landed Crosby, the highest draft pick it had given up was a second-rounder for middle linebacker Roquan Smith.

Title: Getting Started With The Popover API
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Godstime Aburu)
Summary: The article discusses rebuilding tooltips using the browser’s native Popover API instead of relying on JavaScript libraries. It highlights how traditional tooltips often face accessibility and usability issues, which the native API can address by handling opening, closing, keyboard interaction, and accessibility natively. Although still evolving, the Popover API offers a more integrated and standards-compliant approach to tooltips.
Content:
How to Rebuild a Tooltip Using the Browser’s Native Popover API

What happens if you rebuild a single tooltip using the browser’s native model without the aid of a library? The Popover API turns tooltips from something you simulate into something the browser actually understands. Opening and closing, keyboard interaction, Escape handling, and much of the accessibility now come from the platform itself, not from ad-hoc JavaScript.

Tooltips feel like the smallest UI problem you can have. They’re tiny and usually hidden. When someone asks how to build one, the traditional answer almost always comes back using some JavaScript library. And for a long time, that was the sensible advice. I followed it, too.

On the surface, a tooltip is simple. Hover or focus on an element, show a little box with some text, then hide it when the user moves away. But once you ship one to real users, the edges start to show. Keyboard users tab into the trigger but never see the tooltip. Screen readers announce it twice or not at all. The tooltip flickers when you move the mouse too quickly. It overlaps content on smaller screens. Pressing Escape does not close it. Focus gets lost.

Over time, my tooltip code grew into something I didn’t really want to own anymore. Event listeners piled up. Hover and focus had to be handled separately. Outside clicks needed special cases. ARIA attributes had to be kept in sync by hand. Every small fix added another layer of logic. Libraries helped, but they were also more like black boxes I worked around instead of fully understanding what was happening behind the scenes.

That was what pushed me to look at the newer Popover API. I wanted to see what would happen if I rebuilt a single tooltip using the browser’s native model without the aid of a library. As we start, it’s worth noting that, as with any new feature, there are some things with it that are still being ironed out. That said, it currently enjoys great browser support, although several pieces of the overall API are in flux. It’s worth keeping an eye on Can I Use in the meantime.

Before the Popover API, using a tooltip library was not a shortcut. It was the default. Browsers didn’t have a native concept of a tooltip that worked across mouse, keyboard, and assistive technology. If you cared about correctness, your only option was to use a library.

Title: February 2026 Review and Outlook
Author: The Market Intelligence Desk Team
Summary: In February 2026, U.S. equities showed mixed results amid sector rotation. Large-cap indices were restrained, with the Nasdaq 100 declining 2.3% and the cap-weighted S&P 500 falling 0.8%. Conversely, equal-weight and mid-cap indices outperformed, supported by gains in energy, utilities, and industrials. Treasury yields declined, and energy prices rose amid geopolitical risks, reflecting a cautious but broadening market leadership.
Content:
US Markets February 2026 Review and Outlook

The Market Intelligence Desk Team

Market Intelligence Desk by this author

Executive Summary: U.S. equities delivered mixed headline performance amid significant internal rotation. Market leadership broadened as equal-weight, mid-cap, and value exposures outperformed. Market breadth improved, with seven of 11 large-cap sectors within 0.5% of their respective 52-week highs. Mega-cap growth and AI-adjacent software weighed on cap-weighted indices. Treasury yields declined meaningfully, led by the long end. Energy prices strengthened amid geopolitical risk, reinforcing real-asset leadership. Earnings growth remains solid despite increasing dispersion beneath the surface.

U.S. equity markets delivered a mixed performance in February, with headline indices masking significant rotation and dispersion beneath the surface. Among large caps, the Dow Jones Industrials rose modestly by 0.3%, the S&P 500 declined 0.8% for the second time in three months, and the Nasdaq 100 fell 2.3%, posting its worst monthly decline since March 2025. Cap-weighted indices were restrained by continued corrective price action in select large-cap growth stocks, as evidenced by the Magnificent Seven Index, which declined 7.3%, its worst since March 2025. Conversely, the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index gained 3.5%, its best since May 2025, outperforming the cap-weighted S&P 500 for the fourth consecutive month. The S&P Midcap 400 rose 4.1%, its best month since May 2025.

Importantly, February did not resemble a broad risk-off environment. Instead, market action was characterized by rotation rather than liquidation. Investors continued reallocating away from concentrated leadership toward a broader mix of cyclicals and defensives, reflected in strong gains across energy, utilities, materials, staples, industrials, and healthcare. This shift suggests confidence in the durability of economic activity, even as debate persists about the return profile and timing of large-scale AI investment.

From a macro perspective, the backdrop remained supportive but nuanced. Economic data indicated a gradually cooling economy rather than contraction, while inflation progress remained uneven. Treasury yields declined significantly during the month, with the 10-year yield falling below 4%, reinforcing expectations for a patient Federal Reserve. Overall, February appeared less like a turning point for equities and more like an extension of an ongoing transition toward broader participation and more selective leadership.

The divergence in February was largely driven by continued weakness in mega-cap growth, particularly within the technology sector.
